data.title: US Crude Stockpiles Jump in Surprise Build
data.description:
US crude oil inventories rose by 5.27 million barrels in the week ended January 9, 2025, sharply reversing a 2.8 million-barrel build in the prior week.

The increase marked the largest inventory build since late October contrasted with market expectations for a 2 million-barrel draw.

data.title: Even Oil Companies Are Nervous About Trump's Crackdown on Offshore Wind — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Avi Salzman

President Donald Trump has made no secret of his disdain for wind power. He even brought it up at a meeting with oil executives on Friday that was ostensibly about drilling in Venezuela.

"By the way, in case you people don't know, I'm not much of a windmill person," Trump said. "My goal is to not let any windmill be built."

His administration is making good on that promise, forcing work to stop on all offshore wind projects on Dec. 22 because of what officials said was a national security concern.

The Department of the Interior said that the turbines may block radar signals, based on classified reports. On Monday, a judge lifted the stop work order for one project off Rhode Island, but the other four that were under construction remain stalled, losing millions of dollars each day. Each project had already gone through a government permitting process lasting more than five years, which included reviews by defense and security officials.

On its face, Trump's blockage of wind projects looks like a good thing for fossil fuel producers. Wind power competes against resources like coal and natural gas, all of which are used to generate electricity. But oil companies also rely on predictable permitting decisions. And some have already raised concerns that their fossil fuel investments could be derailed by a change in presidential administrations.

"I certainly would like to see those projects that have been permitted in the past continue to be developed," Colette Hirstius, president of Shell USA, told the Financial Times last year. Shell previously walked away from its U.S. offshore wind investments, but sees the permitting uncertainty as a threat to its oil projects too, including in the Gulf of Mexico. "That type of permitting uncertainty has been utilized to undermine the permits that we have in the past," she said.

The American Petroleum Institute, or API, which advocates for the oil and gas industry, has made permitting reform its number one goal this year. "It takes far too long to build the infrastructure required to move energy where it's needed," API President Mike Sommers told reporters. "Red tape, delay, endless litigation, all drive up costs, limit supply, and strain reliability."

But permitting reform is now stalled because of offshore wind. Congress appeared to be closer than ever to passing a bipartisan bill that could speed the permitting process both for fossil fuel projects and renewables.

The bill was also expected to include provisions making it harder for administrations to cancel permits that had already been issued. That wouldn't just help offshore wind — it could also have stopped President Joe Biden from canceling the Keystone XL oil pipeline project during his administration.

But Democrats walked away from the bill last month because of the Trump administration's moratorium on wind projects. They do not want to move forward if the administration is going to unilaterally cancel projects it doesn't like.

Asked by a Barron's reporter on Tuesday about whether API would back offshore wind in order to get a permitting deal, Sommers said his group is working with trade groups for alternative energy.

"It's time for both sides to put their weapons down," he said. "We're going to need... a lot more energy going forward, and that means all sources of energy. We are not in competition with each other."

The oil and gas industry will never fully embrace renewable energy. But to grow faster, it may need to quietly start advocating for those much-maligned windmills.

Laura Sanicola contributed reporting.

Write to Avi Salzman at avi.salzman@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Estrella Immunopharma Disclosed Failure to Satisfy a Continued Listing Rule or Standard
data.description:
On January 7, 2026, the company received a Nasdaq notice that it failed to comply with Listing Rule 5620(a) by not holding an annual shareholder meeting within the required timeframe. The company has 45 days to submit a compliance plan and plans to hold its annual meeting in April 2026; the notice has no immediate effect on trading.

Original SEC Filing: Estrella Immunopharma, Inc. [ ESLA ] - 8-K - Jan. 13, 2026

data.title: BlueScope Steel to Pay $293 Million Special Dividend
data.description:
(21:50 GMT) *BlueScope Steel to Pay A$1.00/Share Special Dividend
(21:51 GMT) *BlueScope Steel: Dividend Returns A$438 Million in Surplus Cash to Shareholders
(21:52 GMT) *BlueScope Steel: Surplus Cash Generated by Sales Including Tata JV Stake, Land
(21:52 GMT) *BlueScope Steel: On-Market Buyback Currently Not Available Due to Corporate Activity, Regulatory Settings
(21:53 GMT) *BlueScope Steel: Dividend Independent of Any Takeover Proposals For the Company
(22:16 GMT) BlueScope Steel to Pay $293 Million Special Dividend

By Rhiannon Hoyle

BlueScope Steel will pay out roughly US$293 million as a special dividend funded by surplus cash from the sale of land and a stake in an Indian joint venture.

The company said Wednesday it will pay shareholders 1.00 Australian dollar per share, equivalent to almost 67 U.S. cents, next month.

BlueScope, which last week rejected a A$13.2-billion takeover bid from Australian conglomerate SGH and U.S. steelmaker Steel Dynamics, said the payout would total A$438 million.

"The board has elected to return this surplus cash to shareholders by way of an unfranked special dividend given that the operation of the on-market buyback is currently not available in light of corporate activity and regulatory settings," BlueScope said.

This dividend "is part of BlueScope's established capital management framework and is independent of any prior or potential future proposals for the company," BlueScope said.

Write to Rhiannon Hoyle at rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com

data.title: South Korea Export Price Growth Slows in December
data.description:
South Korea’s export prices rose 2.3% from a year earlier in December 2025, following a downwardly revised 5.5% increase in a month earlier.

The increase was largely driven by a 2.3% rise in manufactured goods prices, following a 5.5% increase the previous month.

Prices for agricultural, forestry and marine products climbed 7.2% from 7% in November.

Exports to the US continued to be affected by a 15% tariff, though overall export volumes still increased 11.9% from a year earlier.

On a month-on-month basis, export prices rose 1.1%, slowing from a 3.5% gain in November.

data.title: Chevron eyes Texas, Mississippi refineries to run Venezuelan oil, sources say
data.description:
Chevron plans to run Venezuelan crude oil at its Pasadena, Texas and Pascagoula, Mississippi refineries, according to people familiar with operations at the two plants.

No date has been set for Venezuelan crude to arrive at the 118,750 barrel-per-day Pasadena and 356,440-bpd Pascagoula refineries, the sources said.

data.title: EDUCATIONAL DEVELOPMENT CORP SEC 10-Q Report
data.description:
Educational Development Corp (EDC), a prominent player in the educational publishing industry, has released its Form 10-Q report for the third quarter of fiscal 2026. The report outlines significant financial and operational challenges faced by the company, including a notable decline in revenues and active brand partners, as well as strategic plans to address these issues.

Financial Highlights

  • Net Revenues: $7.0 million, decreased by 36.6% compared to the same period last year due to a decline in sales volume and active brand partners.
  • Gross Margin: $4.3 million, representing a decrease of 37.6% from the previous year, primarily due to increased discounts and shipping promotions.
  • Operating Income: $0.7 million, decreased by 50% from the previous year, reflecting lower sales and higher discounts.
  • Net Earnings (Loss): $7.8 million, a significant improvement from a loss of $0.8 million in the prior year, driven by a gain from the sale of the Hilti Complex.
  • Basic and Diluted Earnings (Loss) Per Share: $0.91, compared to a loss of $0.10 per share in the previous year, reflecting improved profitability.

Business Highlights

  • Revenue Segments: The PaperPie division experienced a significant decrease in net revenues, dropping by 36.7% to $6.2 million for the three months ended November 30, 2025, compared to the same period in the previous year. This decline is attributed to a reduction in the number of active brand partners and economic challenges impacting disposable income.
  • Revenue Segments: The Publishing division's net revenues decreased by 38.5% to $0.8 million for the three months ended November 30, 2025, primarily due to a decrease in overall sales volume and increased tariffs affecting product costs.
  • Sales Units: The average number of active brand partners in the PaperPie division decreased significantly to 5,100 during the third quarter of fiscal 2026, down from 12,400 in the same quarter of the previous year. This decline is linked to economic challenges and uncertainties surrounding the Usborne distribution agreement.
  • Future Outlook: The company plans to address ongoing challenges by reducing inventory levels to generate free cash flows and rebuilding the active PaperPie Brand Partner base. Management believes these efforts will help alleviate substantial doubt about the company's ability to continue as a going concern.
  • Future Outlook: Following the sale of the Hilti Complex and the payoff of bank loans, the company intends to reintroduce new titles and enhance its PaperPie e-commerce and "Backoffice" systems to stimulate brand partner engagement and recruitment.
  • Operational Challenges: The company faced challenges due to purchasing restrictions imposed by its lender, which affected the availability of new titles and certain out-of-stock items. These restrictions have since been lifted following the sale of the Hilti Complex.
  • Operational Challenges: The rebranding of the direct sales division from Usborne Books & More to PaperPie, along with unmet purchase requirements under the Usborne distribution agreement, has created uncertainty and impacted brand partner recruitment and retention.

SEC Filing: EDUCATIONAL DEVELOPMENT CORP [ EDUC ] - 10-Q - Jan. 13, 2026

data.title: Belden Files 8K - Other Events >BDC
data.description:
Belden Inc. (BDC) filed a Form 8K - Other Events - with the U.S Securities and Exchange Commission on January 13, 2026.

On January 12, 2026, Belden Inc. (the "Company") issued a press release announcing that it has commenced, subject to market conditions, the issuance and sale of €450 million aggregate principal amount of senior subordinated notes due 2033 (the "Notes Offering") to fund, together with cash on hand, the redemption of all of the outstanding 3.375% senior subordinated notes due 2027 (the "2027 Notes") and to pay related fees and expenses, pursuant to the terms of the Indenture relating to the 2027 Notes. A copy of the press release is attached hereto as Exhibit 99.1 and incorporated herein by reference.

On January 13, 2026, the Company also issued a press release announcing the pricing of the Notes Offering. A copy of the press release is attached hereto as Exhibit 99.2 and incorporated herein by reference.

On January 12, 2026, the Company issued a Notice of Conditional Redemption to holders of the 2027 Notes. The redemption date for the 2027 Notes will be February 11, 2026, conditioned upon the closing of the Notes Offering.

The full text of this SEC filing can be retrieved at: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/913142/000119312526011887/d16286d8k.htm

Any exhibits and associated documents for this SEC filing can be retrieved at: https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/913142/000119312526011887/0001193125-26-011887-index.htm

Public companies must file a Form 8-K, or current report, with the SEC generally within four days of any event that could materially affect a company's financial position or the value of its shares.

data.title: Colombia raises $4.95 billion in its largest ever bond issuance
data.description:
Colombia raised $4.95 billion with the issuance of bonds due 2029, 2031 and 2033, in what is the Andean country's largest such deal, the Finance Ministry said on Tuesday in a statement.

  • The weighted-average coupon in the issuance was at 5.93%, the ministry said.

  • The deal was part of Colombia's financing strategy for this year, it added.

  • Total book order reached as much as about $23.2 billion, according to the statement.

  • The sale included $2 billion in 2029 notes at a 5.375% coupon, $1.475 billion in 2031 bonds at 6.125%, and $1.475 billion in 2033 securities at 6.5%, the Finance Ministry said.

  • More than 290 investors participated, primarily from the United Kingdom (47%), the United States (28%) and Colombia (4%).

data.title: 'All Restraint Is Gone,' Investors Say After Trump's $200 Billion Mortgage-Bond Order — Barrons.com
data.description:
Karishma Vanjani

The Trump administration and the Federal Reserve appear to be flooding the financial system with liquidity, even as officials insist they aren't reviving 'quantitative easing.'

Quantitative easing, or QE as it's called, is an unconventional tool at the heart of the market debate. It refers to the Fed buying large amounts of longer-dated financial assets while keeping interest rates near rock-bottom, all with a single goal: to increase liquidity, stimulate borrowing, and boost investment. QE is usually used during recessions.

Technically, the latest actions on monetary and fiscal policy stemming from Washington don't carry the "QE" label, but investors say the outcome is similar: a flood of liquidity.

Comprehensively, "when you look at it it's like a trillion dollar of stimulus this year," said Daniel Clifton, head of policy research for Strategas Securities. But "I've never called it QE," he added, since neither interest rates are at zero, nor is the Fed buying longer-dated debt.

The bullish logic requires a bit of math. Two government-controlled mortgage giants, Freddie Mac and Fannie Mae, have just been ordered to buy up $200 billion in mortgage-backed securities (MBS) by the president to force down mortgage rates and stimulate home buying. That follows moves to cut red tape and boost credit creation.

By passing the GENIUS Act in July and lowering the capital requirements for banks this year, the government has given major lenders the green light and space to invest more heavily in assets like Treasuries and cryptocurrencies.

Meanwhile, the Fed is "now easing with both hands on the pump," wrote Gavekal's Chief U.S. Economist Will Denyer last month.

The central bank started purchasing short-term U.S. debt or Treasury bills last month, and its balance sheet is noticeably expanding for the first time since 2022, outside the run-up seen in 2023 to rescue banks. When the central bank buys securities, it adds cash to the seller's reserve account held at the Fed, which pumps new money into the financial system. (Paying taxes has the opposite effect, draining liquidity).

The Fed, in aggregate, is estimated to purchase between $220 billion and $300 billion in the first year. It has already bought $54.43 billion in T-bills since Dec. 12. The third interest rate reduction enacted by the Fed last month further adds to the easy money narrative.

"In effect, the Fed handed investors a measured dose of quantitative easing for Christmas, signaling a policy mix that is now unmistakably tilted toward accommodation," Denyer wrote. Fed Chair Jerome Powell, in a press conference last month, said its actions don't constitute as quantitative easing.

The upshot? When there are interventions by the government and actions by the Fed that ease conditions, they add cash to the market that can flow to risky assets. The volume of buying firepower can make investors feel like there's a "floor" under asset prices, pushing them into markets even when valuations look stretched.

"Until something breaks probably [look for] higher stock prices at unsustainable valuations," wrote Michael Lewitt in The Credit Strategist newsletter shortly after Trump's decision on MBS buying. "All pretense of fiscal/monetary restraint is gone."

Extra money in the system can also rekindle inflation, so investors are watching the M2 money supply — a rough gauge of how much cash is circulating in the system. While M2 is currently growing at over 4%, experts like Clifton suggest inflation risks only become serious if that growth hits 6% to 8%.

To be sure, Trump's moves underscore the Republican Party's effort to address affordability and boost the economy ahead of November's mid-term elections — and some would say those goals can be achieved without the dangerous side effects that usually follow poorly timed, reckless government spending.

"Is the MBS purchase QE? In my opinion, the answer is no," said Tan Kai Xian, analyst at Gavekal. "In part because, the money is not 'printed out of thin air.' The Trump administration is simply reallocating resources under its control."

Essentially, the argument is that Fannie and Freddie are deploying existing reserves. Federal Housing Finance Agency Director Bill Pulte told Reuters that each entity holds nearly $100 billion in available funds. Same logic can be applied for the Fed that's swapping T-bills for cash — trading one liquid asset for another.

Still, it is like QE — at least in spirit — as Trump is asking Fannie and Freddie to buy duration and add liquidity to the market, argues Mohit Kumar, chief economist & strategist for Europe at Jefferies.

Trump is also influencing one of the most "sticky" components of household budgets: Mortgages and rent. "Whether these steps succeed remains uncertain, but the intent to tackle energy, housing, and financing head-on is unmistakable," wrote Seth Meyer, portfolio manager at Janus Henderson.

Write to Karishma Vanjani at karishma.vanjani@dowjones.com.

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.
